text: The surgeon begins by minimally mobilizing the colon to access the surgical field. The ipsoras muscle is identified to prevent ureteral injury during the dissection of pyramidal fat. The small artery of the lower pole is dissected first, followed by the identification of the main renal pedicle without placing vessel loops. The perirenal fat is then dissected minimally to expose the tumor.

The surgeon clamps the two renal arteries using bulldog clamps. Cold scissors are used to excise the tumor, and an absolute clip is placed on any visualized arterial branch. During the excision, the collecting system is visibly opened.

The surgeon places a running suture on the tumor bed, securing both ends with absolute clips to initiate parenchymal compression. A second ring suture is applied to the resection bed, contributing significantly to hemostasis by directly suturing vascular branches and closing the collecting system.

The surgeon performs early unclamping by removing the arterial clamp after placing one or two running sutures on the tumor bed, before completing parenchyma repair. The renal replant is finished by placing the last running suture to bring the renal parenchyma together, with absolute clips applied on the wire after each needle passage to pressurize the tissue.
